summary: Reis observes that widespread AI tool adoption is creating 'brain-rotted zombies' - workers and students who have outsourced thinking to ChatGPT, losing their ability to generate original ideas or understand their own output. He warns of a growing 'competency debt bubble' as students who AI'd their way through school enter the workforce unable to actually perform, and calls for a return to fundamentals like writing memos yourself and manually debugging code.
key_points: Coworkers are becoming 'mindless automatons' who toss scattered ideas into ChatGPT instead of thinking through problems
Fall 2025 was the first semester where every student used AI for everything, resulting in widespread failures from 'implausibly bad' submissions
We are accruing 'competency debt' - human debt that will come due when AI-dependent workers are expected to lead
Schools are scrambling to figure out how to teach in the age of AI, with some returning to Blue Books and group discussions
The only vaccine against brain-rot is returning to fundamentals: write the memo yourself, read a book, manually debug code
money_quotes: In this case, AI's not an assistant, and not even a crutch. It's a wholesale lobotomy of entire roles.
I fear we're building a competency debt bubble. Just as we accrue technical debt by cutting corners in code and systems, we are now accruing human debt.
If you can't spot 'implausibly bad' slop, you aren't proficient at AI. You're just a glorified copy-paster who will eventually be replaced by the very tool you're leaning on.
Mindless velocity without comprehension is just a fast track to collective stupidity. Idiocracy was a comedy, not a documentary.
If you stop using your brain, you shouldn't be surprised when it stops working.
key_insight: The rapid adoption of AI tools without maintaining foundational competencies is creating a generation of workers who can use AI but cannot think independently, building a human competency debt that will eventually come due.
